title: Throwing a small stoneware teapot
subtitle: A three-part thrown teapot with strainer spout, flanged lid, and pulled handle, fired to cone 6
excerpt: A complete three-part stoneware teapot: a thrown body of approximately 600 ml volume, a flanged lid with gallery, and a pulled spout with pierced strainer. The handle is pulled from a drawn coil and attached at leather-hard stage. Glaze-fired to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). Requires a potter's wheel and kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage when clay has dried enough to hold its shape and resist fingerprints but remains cool and damp to the touch, firm enough to trim and attach handles
Fully air-dried clay that is uniformly pale, room temperature throughout, and contains no remaining free moisture; ready for bisque firing
The first kiln firing at approximately 1000 degrees C that removes all remaining water and organic material, producing a porous clay body ready to accept glaze
The second kiln firing that melts the glaze into a glassy coating and vitrifies the clay body; for cone 6 stoneware this occurs at approximately 1220 degrees C
A temperature-measurement standard used in ceramics: cone 6 corresponds to approximately 1220 degrees C and is a common target for stoneware
A narrow raised ring trimmed into the base of a thrown piece that lifts the base clear of the kiln shelf and provides a finished lower edge
The hand motion used to raise and thin the walls of a thrown piece, with one hand pressing inward from outside and the other lifting from inside
A narrow horizontal ledge thrown or trimmed inside the rim of a lidded vessel on which the lid flange rests

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln.
A teapot is one of the most demanding thrown forms: it requires three separately thrown components that must fit each other precisely, plus a pulled handle attached at
leather-hard
stage. The body is approximately 600 ml and is thrown with a
gallery
inside the rim to seat the flanged lid. The spout is thrown separately and fitted with pierced strainer holes before attachment.
Glaze-fired
to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). This project builds on the techniques covered in
centring half a kilo of stoneware
throwing a lidded jar in stoneware
, and
pulling and attaching a thrown spout
. Review those before starting.

materials.name: Stoneware clay, smooth
materials.quantity: 1.2 kg (body 600 g, lid 150 g, spout 150 g, handle and trimmings 300 g)

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: as needed for dipping or brushing

content.text: Preparation
A teapot is a system of fitted parts. All three thrown components must be measured with calipers at the throwing stage so the lid gallery, spout opening, and handle attachment points align at
leather-hard

. Throw the body and lid in the same session so they dry at the same rate and can be fitted together while both are leather-hard. Throw the spout in the same session and wrap it separately to slow its drying if needed. Wedge all clay thoroughly before centering.
Throwing the body
Centre 600 g of stoneware on a bat. Open the base to approximately 90 mm diameter, leaving a floor approximately 8 mm thick. Refer to the
centring technique
for the centering sequence.
Pull the walls up using
pulling a wall
technique until the form is approximately 120 mm tall and 110 mm wide at its widest point. The body of a small teapot is a rounded cylinder with a slightly narrowed neck.
Collar the rim inward to a diameter of approximately 60 mm, leaving a wall 6 mm thick at the top. This narrower neck prevents the lid from rattling.
Throw the
gallery

inside the rim: with the wheel turning slowly, use a finger to press inward and slightly downward approximately 8 mm below the rim top to create a ledge approximately 4 mm wide. This ledge is what the lid flange will sit on.
Measure the inside diameter of the gallery with calipers and record the measurement. The lid flange must match this diameter within 2 mm.
Smooth the exterior with the wooden rib. Cut the body from the bat with a wire cutter and leave on the bat to dry to leather-hard before trimming.
Throwing the lid

Centre 150 g of clay. Throw a lid with a flange: open and throw a low dome form approximately 70 mm in overall diameter. On the underside rim, create a downward-projecting flange approximately 4 mm wide that will sit inside the body gallery. The outer diameter of the flange should match the caliper measurement taken from the gallery.

Throw a small knob on the lid top: pull up a small cylinder 15 mm tall from the centre of the lid dome, narrow it slightly, and round the top. The knob must be hollow enough that it does not crack during drying.
Allow the lid to firm to leather-hard before trimming the flange precisely with the loop tool on the banding wheel. Check the fit against the body gallery before it is too dry to adjust.
Throwing the spout

Centre 150 g of clay. Throw a narrow cylinder approximately 80 mm tall and no more than 25 mm in diameter at the base, tapering to approximately 15 mm at the tip. The spout walls should be 4 to 5 mm thick throughout. Refer to the
spout technique
for form and angle guidance.
Leave the spout on the bat to dry to leather-hard alongside the body. Do not allow it to dry faster than the body or it will be too dry to score and attach cleanly.
Trimming and assembly
Trim the body at leather-hard: invert onto the banding wheel and trim a
foot-ring
approximately 8 mm wide and 6 mm tall using the loop tool. The floor inside the foot-ring should be approximately 6 mm thick when finished.

Pierce the strainer holes: decide where the spout will attach on the body side. In that area, pierce a cluster of 9 to 12 holes, each 3 mm in diameter, using the needle tool. These filter out tea leaves. Smooth the interior edges of each hole with a damp sponge.

Cut the base of the spout at the angle it will meet the body: typically 30 to 40 degrees from horizontal so the spout rises at a useful pouring angle. Hold the spout against the body to check the curve of the cut matches the curve of the body wall.

Score and slip both the spout base and the body at the attachment point. Press the spout firmly against the body, checking that the spout tip is level and the spout opening aligns with the strainer holes. Blend the exterior join with a damp finger. Do not seal the join over the strainer holes.

Pull the handle: take approximately 80 g of clay and pull a handle coil by drawing the clay downward repeatedly with a wet hand until you have a flattened band approximately 130 mm long, 18 mm wide, and 8 mm thick. Allow to firm for 15 to 20 minutes until it holds a curve without sagging. Score and slip the two attachment points on the body (opposite the spout, one near the top, one near the bottom). Press the handle into position and blend the joins. Allow the assembled teapot to dry slowly.
Drying and bisque firing
Dry the teapot and lid together, covered loosely with plastic for the first 2 days to allow the joined parts to equalise moisture slowly. Once
bone-dry
, fire to
bisque

at approximately 1000 degrees C. Fire the lid resting on the body in the gallery position: this ensures they sinter together predictably and the fit is maintained through the clay's shrinkage during firing.
Glazing
Wax the foot-ring and the underside of the lid flange with wax resist before glazing. Glaze must not be on any surfaces that will be in contact with the kiln shelf or with each other during the
glaze firing
: glaze melts and will fuse pieces permanently to the shelf or to each other.

Apply the mid-fire glaze to the interior of the body by pouring a cup of glaze inside and swirling to coat all surfaces, then pouring out. Glaze the exterior by dipping, or apply with a mop brush in two or three overlapping coats.
Glaze the lid exterior and the knob. Confirm no raw glaze is on the flange underside or the gallery ledge in the body. Glaze the gallery interior and the body interior as the same piece.
Check the spout interior: it should be glazed on the inside. Glaze can be introduced by pouring a small amount inside and tilting the teapot slowly to coat the inner walls of the spout.
Glaze firing
Fire to
cone

6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). Fire the lid on the body with a thin layer of alumina wash on the gallery ledge to prevent the flange fusing to the body. A standard oxidation firing schedule is appropriate for most mid-fire glazes. Allow the kiln to cool to below 100 degrees C before opening.
Care

Fully glazed stoneware fired to cone 6 is dishwasher safe and microwave safe. The lid should be removed before placing in the dishwasher to prevent rattling damage. The unglazed foot-ring may darken slightly with use; this is normal.
Variations
Gooseneck spout: throw a longer, more slender spout cylinder. Curve it upward at a lower angle to get a pour-over kettle profile.
Side handle: attach a pulled loop handle horizontally on the body side. This gives a Chinese-style side-handled teapot.
Larger volume: use 900 g for the body to produce a 4-cup teapot; scale the spout and handle proportionally.
Two-glaze decoration: glaze the body in one colour and the lid in a contrasting colour to distinguish them visually.

rows.problem: Lid does not fit the gallery after bisque firing
rows.cause: Clay shrinks approximately 10 to 12 percent during firing; if the flange was not trimmed to match the gallery exactly before firing, the difference is magnified
rows.fix: Before firing, always check the fit of the leather-hard lid in the leather-hard body and trim to fit. After bisque, a poorly fitting lid can sometimes be adjusted by grinding the flange lightly with wet-dry paper

rows.cause: Spout tip not cut at the correct angle, or the spout bore is too small
rows.fix: The spout tip should be cut at an angle so the underside of the tip is the lowest point of the spout; water then breaks cleanly rather than running back. A wider bore also reduces dripping

rows.problem: Handle cracking at the attachment points
rows.cause: Handle applied when body clay was too dry, preventing a proper bond; or handle dried faster than the body
rows.fix: Both body and handle attachment must be at the same leather-hard stage. Wrap the body in damp cloth if it has dried faster than the handle. Score deeply and use thick slip at both attachment points

rows.problem: Lid fusing to the body in the glaze firing
rows.cause: Glaze or excess slip on the gallery ledge or flange underside
rows.fix: Use alumina wash on the gallery ledge before the glaze firing. If pieces fuse, do not force them apart; gentle tapping with a wooden mallet sometimes separates them without breakage
